stem: Define a sequence by \( x_1 = 1 \) and \( x_{n+1} = \sqrt{[[a]] + x_n} \) for \( n \ge 1 \). This sequence is increasing and bounded above, so it converges. Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n \).
solution: The limit \( L \) is the positive fixed point of \( L = \sqrt{[[a]] + L} \), so \( L^2 - L - [[a]] = 0 \). The positive root is \( L = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 4\cdot[[a]]}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the negative root of \( L^2 - L - [[a]] = 0 \), which the (positive) sequence cannot converge to.
This is one less than the correct fixed point.
This is one more than the correct fixed point.
This mistakes the constant \( [[a]] \) inside the radical for the limit itself.
This doubles the correct fixed point.
This is two more than the correct fixed point.

stem: Define a sequence by \( x_1 = [[msq]] \) and \( x_{n+1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(x_n + \dfrac{[[msq]]}{x_n}\right) \) for \( n \ge 1 \). This sequence is decreasing and bounded below, so it converges. Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n \).
solution: This is Newton's (Babylonian) averaging iteration for \( \sqrt{[[msq]]} \): any fixed point \( L \) satisfies \( L = \tfrac12\left(L + \tfrac{[[msq]]}{L}\right) \), i.e. \( L^2 = [[msq]] \), so \( L = [[answer]] \) (the positive root).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the starting value \( x_1 \) with the limit; the sequence decreases strictly below it.
This is one more than the correct limit.
This is one less than the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.
This halves the starting value instead of taking a square root.
This is two more than the correct limit.

stem: Define a sequence by \( x_1 = 1 \) and \( x_{n+1} = [[c]]\sqrt{x_n} \) for \( n \ge 1 \). This sequence is increasing and bounded above, so it converges. Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n \).
solution: A fixed point \( L \) satisfies \( L = [[c]]\sqrt{L} \), so \( \sqrt{L} = [[c]] \) (discarding \( L=0 \), which the sequence never reaches), giving \( L = [[c]]^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the multiplier \( [[c]] \) in the recursion with the limit itself.
This doubles the multiplier instead of squaring it.
This is one more than the correct limit.
This is one less than the correct limit.
This halves the correct limit.
This is one more than the multiplier, not the limit.

stem: Define a sequence by \( x_1 = 0 \) and \( x_{n+1} = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}x_n + [[b]] \) for \( n \ge 1 \). This sequence is increasing and bounded above, so it converges. Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n \).
solution: A fixed point \( L \) satisfies \( L = \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}L + [[b]] \), so \( L\left(1 - \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right) = [[b]] \), giving \( L = \dfrac{[[b]]\,[[q]]}{[[q]]-[[p]]} = [[answer]] \). Since \( 0 < [[p]]/[[q]] < 1 \), the map is a contraction and the sequence converges monotonically to \( L \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the additive constant \( [[b]] \), ignoring the contraction factor entirely.
This inverts the fraction \( [[p]]/[[q]] \) when solving for \( L \).
This swaps \( p \) and \( q \) in the numerator of the fixed-point formula.
This adds the contraction factor to 1 instead of subtracting it.
This is one more than the correct limit.
This is one less than the correct limit.

stem: Define a sequence by \( x_1 = [[x1]] \) and \( x_{n+1} = [[a]] - \dfrac{[[b]]}{x_n} \) for \( n \ge 1 \). This sequence is increasing and bounded above, so it converges. Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n \).
solution: A fixed point \( L \) satisfies \( L = [[a]] - \dfrac{[[b]]}{L}\), i.e. \( L^2 - [[a]]L + [[b]] = 0 \), with roots \( \dfrac{[[a]] \pm \sqrt{[[a]]^2 - 4\cdot[[b]]}}{2} \). Since \( x_1 = [[x1]] \) lies between the two roots and the map is increasing, the sequence converges up to the LARGER root, \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the smaller root of the fixed-point equation, which the sequence increases past, not toward.
This uses only the linear coefficient \( [[a]] \), skipping the quadratic formula entirely.
This halves \( [[a]] \) without accounting for the discriminant term.
This confuses the starting value \( x_1 \) with the limit.
This is one more than the correct limit.
This is one less than the correct limit.
